data.title: Knorr-Bremse Target Raised to EUR92 From EUR91 by Warburg Research
Knorr-Bremse Raised to Buy From Hold by Warburg Research
Indonesia's EU Trade Deal Unlikely to Boost Long-Term Prospects — Market Talk
data.description:
0935 GMT - Indonesia's trade deal with the European Union isn't expected to provide much of a boost to the country's long-term prospects, economists at Capital Economics write in a note. The agreement, signed earlier this week, offered a respite for the Indonesian government, which has been on the defensive with investors over its policy direction. The economists note that the EU will remove tariffs on more than 90% of Indonesian goods following the deal's implementation. Palm oil and other commodity exports stand to benefit, as well as the textile sector. However, CE cautions that Indonesia's palm oil industry still faces risks from the EU's deforestation regulation, which could significantly disrupt exports to the bloc. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0916 GMT - The U.S. dollar could struggle to rise much further as investors turn cautious ahead of next week's key jobs data, BlueBay Asset Management CIO Mark Dowding says in a note. The dollar rose to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. data. It had been on the verge of breaking out of its recent trading range but has since stalled. "This could infer further sideways price action in the short term," he says. Weak jobs data next week could give a renewed impetus towards a weaker dollar, which is in line with BlueBay's medium-term view. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.373, having hit a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0851 GMT - Eurozone government bond supply is set to decelerate in October from September as it slows down towards the end of the year, Barclays' rates strategists say in a note. For October, they project gross government bond supply of 116 billion euros, down from around 127 billion euros in September, as the pace of syndicated supply slows, they say. Redemptions will rise to 118 billion euros in October from 52 billion euros in September, they forecast. Eurozone sovereigns have issued around 1.173 trillion euros in government bonds, approximately 84% of Barclays' supply projection for the year. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0824 GMT - The euro is slightly firmer against the U.S. dollar, while eurozone 10-year government bond yields edge marginally lower. The eurozone data calendar is thin ahead of end-of-month flurry of data, but there is an Italian government bond auction. RBC Capital Markets expects a continued appreciation of the euro, without this prompting further European Central Bank rate cuts. "We reiterate our call that we have seen the bottom of the ECB's rate cut cycle already," they say. The euro rises 0.1% to $1.1679, stabilizing after sharp falls following Thursday's above-forecast U.S. data. Eurozone bond yields fall by up to 1 basis point, with the 10-year German Bund yield down 0.5 basis points at 2.764%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - Singapore's manufacturing performance is expected to be volatile for the rest of the year, DBS senior economist Chua Han Teng writes in an email. Manufacturing output dropped by 7.8% on year in August, ending 13 straight months of expansion and marking the sharpest decline since March 2024. August's contraction was partly due to adverse base effects, Chua says. The city-state's export-oriented factories face downside risks from a highly uncertain global economic landscape. "External demand remains threatened and challenged by ongoing U.S. tariffs, although the global semiconductor cycle is still supported by structural AI advancement," Chua says. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0809 GMT - U.S. President Trump's new round of sectoral tariff are likely to negatively affect highly trade-dependent economies like Singapore, DBS senior economist Chua Han Teng writes in an email. The measures, including a 100% tariff on branded or patented drug imports from Oct. 1, marks a continuation of Trump's protectionist agenda, he says. "Continued imposition of U.S. tariffs and ongoing uncertainty of new ones will hurt already weakened business sentiment," Chua says. He notes, however, the exemption for drugmakers building a production facility in the U.S. may mitigate the blow. Singapore hosts nine of the world's top 10 drugmakers, with major pharma firms already establishing or expanding manufacturing operations in the U.S., Chua notes.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0755 GMT - Yields on 10-year and 30-year U.K. government bonds, or gilts, rise to three-week highs after weak demand at this week's gilt auctions and amid heightened concern about the fragile state of U.K. public finances. Investors are worried that pressure on the Labour government could push borrowing too high when the autumn budget is announced on November 26. Gilt yields have been rising as "investor doubts grew about the country's fiscal position, amidst calls from some within the governing Labour Party for Prime Minister Starmer to ease the fiscal rules," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. The 10-year gilt yield rises to a high of 4.767%, while the 30-year yield reaches 5.582%, Tradeweb data show. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0732 GMT - China's new 2035 target of 3,600GW of solar and wind power capacity looks conservative and will likely be exceeded, Deutsche Bank analyst Gary Zhou says. He expects supply-side reform to remain the near-term focus, as restoring supply-demand balance in the renewable sector is key for investors. On the demand side, Zhou forecasts China will add 1,455GW of solar and 600GW of wind power capacities during the 2026-2030 period, well above additions in 2021-2025. DB's top renewables pick is GCL Tech, citing its low valuation and potential gains from solar supply reforms. Shares last traded at HK$1.27. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0729 GMT - Bitcoin steadies after sliding to a three-week low on Thursday as strong U.S. data reduced prospects of interest-rate cuts. This lifted Treasury yields and the dollar, causing bitcoin to dip below $109,000 and over $160 billion of crypto-market value to be erased, says Deus x pay's James Madden in a note. Less urgency for rate cuts has "dampened speculative appetite in the crypto market," he says. However, attention now turns to U.S. PCE inflation and revised University of Michigan consumer sentiment data at 1230 GMT and 1400 GMT, respectively. Softer data could enable bitcoin to rebound, although strong inflation could pressure it further, Madden says. Bitcoin rises 0.4% to $109,613, having hit a low of $108,715 on Thursday, LSEG data show. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0725 GMT - The Chinese yuan's recent stability is unlikely to be sustained over the longer term, Barclays analysts say in a research note. China's stronger yuan fixings around 7.10-7.11 over recent weeks have helped keep volatility low, but this is unlikely to be sustained due to near-term risks, including weak economic data amid tariff tensions. "Too much CNY stability or artificially low volatility could also be problematic" if it leads to complacency among exporters and importers on hedging activity, the bank says. USD/CNY is last at 7.14. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0710 GMT - EUR/USD's pullback from last week's high appears to have gathered considerable pace, based on charts, says Quek Ser Leang of UOB's Global Economics & Markets Research in a research report. The currency pair's fall over the past two sessions was the biggest two-session drop since late July, the senior technical strategist notes. Adding to the bearish momentum, the daily moving average convergence divergence indicator has turned noticeably lower, the strategist says. A key level to watch on downside is 1.1610 where two major supports converge, the rising trendline connecting May and August lows and the bottom of the daily Ichimoku cloud, the strategist adds. EUR/USD is 0.1% higher at 1.1679. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0701 GMT - The dollar turns lower after President Trump announced a new round of tariffs, including a 100% tariff on drugs by companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. as well as levies on large trucks and home goods, beginning Oct. 1. The dollar had jumped to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. economic data, including a sizeable upgrade to U.S. second-quarter GDP. This damped prospects of interest-rate cuts. However, Commerzbank analyst Michael Pfister says the U.S. economy's dependence on private consumption poses a risk if sentiment declines. Trump's "erratic trade policy is doing much to dampen consumer sentiment," he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.409 after hitting a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0935 GMT - Indonesia's trade deal with the European Union isn't expected to provide much of a boost to the country's long-term prospects, economists at Capital Economics write in a note. The agreement, signed earlier this week, offered a respite for the Indonesian government, which has been on the defensive with investors over its policy direction. The economists note that the EU will remove tariffs on more than 90% of Indonesian goods following the deal's implementation. Palm oil and other commodity exports stand to benefit, as well as the textile sector. However, CE cautions that Indonesia's palm oil industry still faces risks from the EU's deforestation regulation, which could significantly disrupt exports to the bloc. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0916 GMT - The U.S. dollar could struggle to rise much further as investors turn cautious ahead of next week's key jobs data, BlueBay Asset Management CIO Mark Dowding says in a note. The dollar rose to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. data. It had been on the verge of breaking out of its recent trading range but has since stalled. "This could infer further sideways price action in the short term," he says. Weak jobs data next week could give a renewed impetus towards a weaker dollar, which is in line with BlueBay's medium-term view. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.373, having hit a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0851 GMT - Eurozone government bond supply is set to decelerate in October from September as it slows down towards the end of the year, Barclays' rates strategists say in a note. For October, they project gross government bond supply of 116 billion euros, down from around 127 billion euros in September, as the pace of syndicated supply slows, they say. Redemptions will rise to 118 billion euros in October from 52 billion euros in September, they forecast. Eurozone sovereigns have issued around 1.173 trillion euros in government bonds, approximately 84% of Barclays' supply projection for the year. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0824 GMT - The euro is slightly firmer against the U.S. dollar, while eurozone 10-year government bond yields edge marginally lower. The eurozone data calendar is thin ahead of end-of-month flurry of data, but there is an Italian government bond auction. RBC Capital Markets expects a continued appreciation of the euro, without this prompting further European Central Bank rate cuts. "We reiterate our call that we have seen the bottom of the ECB's rate cut cycle already," they say. The euro rises 0.1% to $1.1679, stabilizing after sharp falls following Thursday's above-forecast U.S. data. Eurozone bond yields fall by up to 1 basis point, with the 10-year German Bund yield down 0.5 basis points at 2.764%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - Singapore's manufacturing performance is expected to be volatile for the rest of the year, DBS senior economist Chua Han Teng writes in an email. Manufacturing output dropped by 7.8% on year in August, ending 13 straight months of expansion and marking the sharpest decline since March 2024. August's contraction was partly due to adverse base effects, Chua says. The city-state's export-oriented factories face downside risks from a highly uncertain global economic landscape. "External demand remains threatened and challenged by ongoing U.S. tariffs, although the global semiconductor cycle is still supported by structural AI advancement," Chua says. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0809 GMT - U.S. President Trump's new round of sectoral tariff are likely to negatively affect highly trade-dependent economies like Singapore, DBS senior economist Chua Han Teng writes in an email. The measures, including a 100% tariff on branded or patented drug imports from Oct. 1, marks a continuation of Trump's protectionist agenda, he says. "Continued imposition of U.S. tariffs and ongoing uncertainty of new ones will hurt already weakened business sentiment," Chua says. He notes, however, the exemption for drugmakers building a production facility in the U.S. may mitigate the blow. Singapore hosts nine of the world's top 10 drugmakers, with major pharma firms already establishing or expanding manufacturing operations in the U.S., Chua notes.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0755 GMT - Yields on 10-year and 30-year U.K. government bonds, or gilts, rise to three-week highs after weak demand at this week's gilt auctions and amid heightened concern about the fragile state of U.K. public finances. Investors are worried that pressure on the Labour government could push borrowing too high when the autumn budget is announced on November 26. Gilt yields have been rising as "investor doubts grew about the country's fiscal position, amidst calls from some within the governing Labour Party for Prime Minister Starmer to ease the fiscal rules," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. The 10-year gilt yield rises to a high of 4.767%, while the 30-year yield reaches 5.582%, Tradeweb data show. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0732 GMT - China's new 2035 target of 3,600GW of solar and wind power capacity looks conservative and will likely be exceeded, Deutsche Bank analyst Gary Zhou says. He expects supply-side reform to remain the near-term focus, as restoring supply-demand balance in the renewable sector is key for investors. On the demand side, Zhou forecasts China will add 1,455GW of solar and 600GW of wind power capacities during the 2026-2030 period, well above additions in 2021-2025. DB's top renewables pick is GCL Tech, citing its low valuation and potential gains from solar supply reforms. Shares last traded at HK$1.27. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0729 GMT - Bitcoin steadies after sliding to a three-week low on Thursday as strong U.S. data reduced prospects of interest-rate cuts. This lifted Treasury yields and the dollar, causing bitcoin to dip below $109,000 and over $160 billion of crypto-market value to be erased, says Deus x pay's James Madden in a note. Less urgency for rate cuts has "dampened speculative appetite in the crypto market," he says. However, attention now turns to U.S. PCE inflation and revised University of Michigan consumer sentiment data at 1230 GMT and 1400 GMT, respectively. Softer data could enable bitcoin to rebound, although strong inflation could pressure it further, Madden says. Bitcoin rises 0.4% to $109,613, having hit a low of $108,715 on Thursday, LSEG data show. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0725 GMT - The Chinese yuan's recent stability is unlikely to be sustained over the longer term, Barclays analysts say in a research note. China's stronger yuan fixings around 7.10-7.11 over recent weeks have helped keep volatility low, but this is unlikely to be sustained due to near-term risks, including weak economic data amid tariff tensions. "Too much CNY stability or artificially low volatility could also be problematic" if it leads to complacency among exporters and importers on hedging activity, the bank says. USD/CNY is last at 7.14. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0710 GMT - EUR/USD's pullback from last week's high appears to have gathered considerable pace, based on charts, says Quek Ser Leang of UOB's Global Economics & Markets Research in a research report. The currency pair's fall over the past two sessions was the biggest two-session drop since late July, the senior technical strategist notes. Adding to the bearish momentum, the daily moving average convergence divergence indicator has turned noticeably lower, the strategist says. A key level to watch on downside is 1.1610 where two major supports converge, the rising trendline connecting May and August lows and the bottom of the daily Ichimoku cloud, the strategist adds. EUR/USD is 0.1% higher at 1.1679. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0701 GMT - The dollar turns lower after President Trump announced a new round of tariffs, including a 100% tariff on drugs by companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. as well as levies on large trucks and home goods, beginning Oct. 1. The dollar had jumped to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. economic data, including a sizeable upgrade to U.S. second-quarter GDP. This damped prospects of interest-rate cuts. However, Commerzbank analyst Michael Pfister says the U.S. economy's dependence on private consumption poses a risk if sentiment declines. Trump's "erratic trade policy is doing much to dampen consumer sentiment," he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.409 after hitting a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

data.title: Why Meta Thinks It Can Challenge Apple in Consumer AI Devices — Heard on the Street — WSJ
data.description:
By Dan Gallagher

There's a popular saying in Silicon Valley that hardware is hard. In the case of Meta Platforms, it's also superexpensive.

The parent company of Facebook and Instagram has generated a cumulative operating loss of a little more than $68 billion over the past four years in the segment that it calls Reality Labs, and that is just the amount investors know about. Meta didn't start disclosing financial results for its hardware-focused business until 2021 — seven years after it spent $2 billion to acquire virtual reality device maker Oculus.

That business has produced a few modest hits, but not enough to stem the red ink. Meta has shipped about 2.8 million units of its Ray-Ban smartglasses over the past four quarters, according to IDC. That is a decent number, but still only about 1% of what the market-research firm estimates for Apple's highly profitable iPhone shipments over the same period. And the new smartglasses that Meta unveiled last week aren't expected to change that course either; analysts project nearly $20 billion in operating losses for the Reality Labs segment this year.

But Meta is playing a long game with a very ambitious target. The company apparently believes that Apple's dominance of consumer devices is vulnerable in the AI age. Chief Executive Mark Zuckerberg sees smartglasses as crucial to his vision for an amped-up version of artificial intelligence called "superintelligence," defined as AI that surpasses human intelligence.

They aren't there yet. But AI is giving a boost to the capabilities of smartglasses, which began as basically face-mounted cameras. Meta's latest glasses called Ray-Ban Display add features like live captioning, which displays text of the person talking to you in a noisy room. Future smartglasses backed by more powerful AI models could theoretically provide full contextual awareness, where a user could do something like look at an unassembled piece of furniture and immediately get step-by-step directions on how to put it together.

"We designed our glasses to be able to empower people with new capabilities as soon as they become possible," Zuckerberg said in introducing the new devices last week.

That's another way of saying that Meta's glasses are well ahead of their time. The new Ray-Ban Display projects data right into the lens and includes an innovative wrist band allowing users to control the device with hand gestures. The new specs drew a lot of interest — especially with a $799 intro price that's far cheaper than Apple's $3,500 Vision Pro headset. But Meta's specs may also need more polish, given that Zuckerberg's live demo had some notable glitches on stage.

Still, given the company's substantial investments in AI, Zuckerberg is right to also pursue new ways that users may interact with it. Smartphones and personal computers are the main portals to AI now, but that could shift as the technology unlocks new capabilities not well suited to those devices. Meta isn't the only company working on this idea. OpenAI has acqui-hired famed former Apple designer Jony Ive to build a line of "AI companions" that the company has described as a "third core device" besides the PC and smartphone.

These newcomers to hardware would seem outmatched. Apple has many decades of experience building sharply designed and very profitable hardware products. But the company formerly known as Facebook has some advantages as well, including the ability to put its products in front of a massive daily user base of nearly 3.5 billion people. And the fat profits generated by Meta's core advertising business can cover a lot of bets; Meta's total operating margin was 44% for the 12-month period ended in June — 12 percentage points ahead of Apple's for the same period, despite Meta's steep losses in its Reality Labs division.

Meta has also been much bolder in its shift to AI, helped by Zuckerberg's enthusiasm and total control of the company's voting shares. Meta is vastly outspending the much larger Apple in both R&D and capital expenditures and owns more core AI technology, such as its Llama family of large language models.

Brian Nowak of Morgan Stanley said Meta's new glasses show the importance of the company's "full stack approach" to building an AI platform. He added that Meta's "leading proprietary hardware, AI native integration and unique data sets are key differentiators to capture this outsized opportunity," in a note to clients.

Face computers from the owner of Facebook may yet turn out to be a natural fit.

Write to Dan Gallagher at dan.gallagher@wsj.com
